book: On the Duties of the Clergy.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Dogmatic Treatises, Ethical Works, and Sermons.
On the Duties of the Clergy.

Chapter XXII. Virtue must never be given up for the sake of a friend. If, however, one has to bear witness against a friend, it must be done with caution. Between friends what candour is needed in opening the heart, what magnanimity in suffering, what freedom in finding fault! Friendship is the guardian of virtues, which are not to be found but in men of like character. It must be mild in rebuking and averse to seeking its own advantage; whence it happens that true friends are scarce among the rich. What is the dignity of friendship? The treachery of a friend, as it is worse, so it is also more hateful than another's, as is recognized from the example of Judas and of Job's friends.

metadata.author: Dogmatic Treatises, Ethical Works, and Sermons

title: Chapter XXII. Virtue must never be given up for the sake of a friend. If, however, one has to bear witness against a friend, it must be done with caution. Between friends what candour is needed in opening the heart, what magnanimity in suffering, what freedom in finding fault! Friendship is the guardian of virtues, which are not to be found but in men of like character. It must be mild in rebuking and averse to seeking its own advantage; whence it happens that true friends are scarce among the rich. What is the dignity of friendship? The treachery of a friend, as it is worse, so it is also more hateful than another's, as is recognized from the example of Judas and of Job's friends.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Nothing, then, must be set before virtue; and that it may never be set aside by the desire for friendship, Scripture also gives us a warning on the subject of friendship.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: There are, indeed various questions raised among philosophers; for instance whether a man ought for the sake of a friend to plot against his country or not, so as to serve his friend?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Whether it is right to break one’s faith, and so aid and maintain a friend’s advantage?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: And Scripture also says: “A maul, and a sword, and a sharp arrow, so is a man that beareth false witness against his friend.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But note what it adds.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It blames not witness given against a friend, but false witness.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For what if the cause of God or of one’s country compels one to give witness?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Ought friendship to take a higher place than our religion, or our love for our fellow-citizens?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: In these matters, however, true witness is required so that a friend may not be assailed by the treachery of a friend, by whose good faith he ought to be acquitted.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: A man, then, ought never to please a friend who desires evil, or to plot against one who is innocent.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Certainly, if it is necessary to give witness, then, when one knows of any fault in a friend, one ought to rebuke him secretly—if he does not listen, one must do it openly.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For rebukes are good, and often better than a silent friendship.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Even if a friend thinks himself hurt, still rebuke him; and if the bitterness of the correction wounds his mind, still rebuke him and fear not.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: “The wounds of a friend are better than the kisses of flatterers.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Rebuke, then, thy erring friend; forsake not an innocent one.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For friendship ought to be steadfast and to rest firm in true affection.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: We ought not to change our friends in childish fashion at some idle fancy.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Open thy breast to a friend that he may be faithful to thee, and that thou mayest receive from him the delight of thy life.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: “For a faithful friend is the medicine of life and the grace of immortality.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Give way to a friend as to an equal, and be not ashamed to be beforehand with thy friend in doing kindly duties.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For friendship knows nothing of pride.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: So the wise man says: “Do not blush to greet a friend.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Do not desert a friend in time of need, nor forsake him nor fail him, for friendship is the support of life.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Let us then bear our burdens as the Apostle has taught: for he spoke to those whom the charity of the same one body had embraced together.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: If friends in prosperity help friends, why do they not also in times of adversity offer their support?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Let us aid by giving counsel, let us offer our best endeavours, let us sympathize with them with all our heart.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: If necessary, let us endure for a friend even hardship.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Often enmity has to be borne for the sake of a friend’s innocence; oftentimes revilings, if one defends and answers for a friend who is found fault with and accused.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Do not be afraid of such displeasure, for the voice of the just says: “Though evil come upon me, I will endure it for a friend’s sake.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: In adversity, too, a friend is proved, for in prosperity all seem to be friends.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: But as in adversity patience and endurance are needed, so in prosperity strong influence is wanted to check and confute the arrogance of a friend who becomes overbearing.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: How nobly Job when he was in adversity said: “Pity me, my friends, pity me.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: That is not a cry as it were of misery, but rather one of blame.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For when he was unjustly reproached by his friends, he answered: “Pity me, my friends,” that is, ye ought to show pity, but instead ye assail and overwhelm a man with whose sufferings ye ought to show sympathy for friendship’s sake.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Preserve, then, my sons, that friendship ye have begun with your brethren, for nothing in the world is more beautiful than that.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is indeed a comfort in this life to have one to whom thou canst open thy heart, with whom thou canst share confidences, and to whom thou canst entrust the secrets of thy heart.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is a comfort to have a trusty man by thy side, who will rejoice with thee in prosperity, sympathize in troubles, encourage in persecution.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What good friends those Hebrew children were whom the flames of the fiery furnace did not separate from their love of each other!

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Of them we have already spoken.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Holy David says well: “Saul and Jonathan were lovely and pleasant, inseparable in their life, in death they were not divided.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: This is the fruit of friendship; and so faith may not be put aside for the sake of friendship.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: He cannot be a friend to a man who has been unfaithful to God.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Friendship is the guardian of pity and the teacher of equality, so as to make the superior equal to the inferior, and the inferior to the superior.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For there can be no friendship between diverse characters, and so the good-will of either ought to be mutually suited to the other.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Let not authority be wanting to the inferior if the matter demands it, nor humility to the superior.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Let him listen to the other as though he were of like position—an equal, and let the other warn and reprove like a friend, not from a desire to show off, but with a deep feeling of love.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Let not thy warning be harsh, nor thy rebuke bitter, for as friendship ought to avoid flattery, so, too, ought it to be free from arrogance.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For what is a friend but a partner in love, to whom thou unitest and attachest thy soul, and with whom thou blendest so as to desire from being two to become one; to whom thou entrustest thyself as to a second self, from whom thou fearest nothing, and from whom thou demandest nothing dishonourable for the sake of thine own advantage.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Friendship is not meant as a source of revenue, but is full of seemliness, full of grace.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Friendship is a virtue, not a way of making money.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: It is produced, not by money, but by esteem; not by the offer of rewards, but by a mutual rivalry in doing kindnesses.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Lastly, the friendships of the poor are generally better than those of the rich, and often the rich are without friends, whilst the poor have many.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For true friendship cannot exist where there is lying flattery.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Many try fawningly to please the rich, but no one cares to make pretence to a poor man.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Whatsoever is stated to a poor man is true, his friendship is free from envy.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: What is more precious than friendship which is shared alike by angels and by men?

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Wherefore the Lord Jesus says: “Make to yourselves friends of the mammon of unrighteousness, that they may receive you into eternal habitations.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: God Himself makes us friends instead of servants, as He Himself says: “Ye are My friends if ye do whatsoever I command you.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: He gave us a pattern of friendship to follow.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: We are to fulfil the wish of a friend, to unfold to him our secrets which we hold in our own hearts, and are not to disregard his confidences.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Let us show him our heart and he will open his to us.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Therefore He says: “I have called you friends, for I have made known unto you all things whatsoever I have heard of My Father.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: A friend, then, if he is a true one, hides nothing; he pours forth his soul as the Lord Jesus poured forth the mysteries of His Father.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: So he who does the will of God is His friend and is honoured with this name.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: He who is of one mind with Him, he too is His friend.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: For there is unity of mind in friends, and no one is more hateful than the man that injures friendship.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Hence in the traitor the Lord found this the worst point on which to condemn his treachery, namely, that he gave no sign of gratitude and had mingled the poison of malice at the table of friendship.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: So He says: “It was thou, a man of like mind, My guide and Mine acquaintance, who ever didst take pleasant meals with Me.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: That is: it could not be endured, for thou didst fall upon Him Who granted grace to thee.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: “For if My enemy had reproached Me I could have borne it, and I would have hid Myself from him who hated Me.”

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: An enemy can be avoided; a friend cannot, if he desires to lay a plot.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Let us guard against him to whom we do not entrust our plans; we cannot guard against him to whom we have already entrusted them.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: And so to show up all the hatefulness of the sin He did not say: Thou, My servant, My apostle; but thou, a man of like mind with Me; that is: thou art not My but thy own betrayer, for thou didst betray a man of like mind with thyself.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: The Lord Himself, when He was displeased with the three princes who had not deferred to holy Job, wished to pardon them through their friend, so that the prayer of friendship might win remission of sins.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Therefore Job asked and God pardoned.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Friendship helped them whom arrogance had harmed.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: These things I have left with you, my children, that you may guard them in your minds—you yourselves will prove whether they will be of any advantage.

verses.book: On the Duties of the Clergy.
verses.text: Meanwhile they offer you a large number of examples, for almost all the examples drawn from our forefathers, and also many a word of theirs, are included within these three books; so that, although the language may not be graceful, yet a succession of old-time examples set down in such small compass may offer much instruction.
